text: A reusable D port is introduced into the anus of a sixty-nine-year-old female patient with adenocarcinoma located 5.5 centimeters from the anal margin. A butterfly needle is inserted intraluminally for ICG injections at the nine, twelve, and three o'clock positions peritumorally, followed by injections into the rectal mucosa and tumor. The needle is then removed. Abdominal laparoscopy reveals ICG fluorescence diffusion around the rectum and along the lymphatic vessels, with vascular dissection performed laparoscopically.

Monocurved reusable instruments are inserted into the transanal D-port to begin transanal minimally invasive surgery. A purse-string suture is placed one centimeter below the tumor using submucosal bites with PDS-II zero, and an extracorporeal knot is tied to close the lumen. A monocurved coagulating hook is used to tattoo the mucosal dissection area. The surgeon initiates full-thickness rectal wall perforation posteriorly, with pneumo-retrorectum enhancing the dissected space and revealing ICG fluorescence. Dissection continues with monocurved grasping forceps and coagulating hook, progressing from posteriorly to laterally and anteriorly.

The surgeon uses ICG fluorescence to reveal and dissect the lower part of the recto-sacral ligament until the pre-sacral fascia is identified. The presacral fascia is carefully freed from the visceral rectal fascia and kept inferiorly. Fluorescence control confirms ICG presence above the dissected plane, facilitating the liberation of the mesorectum from the presacral fascia. Dissection proceeds posteriorly and laterally, with ergonomic instrument use aiding the process. Anterior dissection involves freeing the rectum from the vagina within the rectovaginal septum, with ICG visible in the area, and the vagina is gently pushed anteriorly. Lateral dissection continues with caution to avoid injuring the splanchnic nerves.

The surgeon isolates, clips, and divides the right inferior rectal artery. The dissection proceeds anteriorly until the abdominal scope is visible, while posteriorly it concludes at the promontory. Anteriorly, the rectum and surrounding adipose tissue are kept inferiorly, guided by the fluorescent plane of dissection. The peritoneal sheet is perforated anteriorly first, reaching Douglas' pouch, followed by perforations at the promontory and laterally on both sides.

The final view of the total mesorectal excision (TME) is displayed. A plastic protection is inserted into the D-port, and the rectum is placed inside it. The specimen is removed transanally with the monocurve grasper, after protecting the orifice with plastic protection. The descending colon is cut, and a circular anvil is introduced into the colic lumen and pushed into the pelvis. The D-port is repositioned, and the rectal stump is closed using a PDS-II-Zero running suture. Four thickness bites are placed under transanal and laparoscopic scope control for the purse-string suture. The monocurved anvil grasping forceps hold the spike of the anvil in the middle of the rectal stump during the extracorporeal knot realization. The rectal stump is closed around the spike of the anvil, and the suture is cut. The D-port is removed, and the circular stapler is inserted and connected to the anvil. The anastomosis is performed under abdominal laparoscopic control, followed by a final endoluminal view of the anastomosis.
